column1: Summary
column30: Description

column1: Epic 2 -- AI RCA System MVP
Epic 1 -- AWS Infrastructure Provisioning and Configuration
E2-12: End-to-end integration — load-test, alert, triage, LLM, RCA email
column30:
As a developer, I can trigger a real alert and see the full pipeline execute end-to-end so that the MVP is proven. This is the Sprint 2 demo gate.

Acceptance Criteria:
- load-test.sh triggers latency alert (p95 > 500ms/5m)
- Layer 1: Grafana fires webhook to :8090 — verified in logs
- Layer 2: Triage deduplicates, checks history, decides investigate
- Layer 3: Ollama queries MCP bridges — logs show calls to Prometheus, Loki, Jaeger, Drain3, RCA History
- Structured JSON verdict: ESCALATE or DISMISS with RCA
- On ESCALATE: email received with Drain3 annotations, evidence, suggested actions
- Decision stored in SQLite — visible at GET /decisions
- Total pipeline latency under 60 seconds
- Second identical alert within 5 min: deduplicated (no second LLM call)
- RCA History MCP: second investigation references first RCA

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)
Milestone: Sprint 2 complete

column1: E2-11: Docker-compose for AI stack — triage + 5 MCP servers + Ollama
column30:
As a developer, I can containerize the full AI triage stack with docker-compose so that it deploys reproducibly.

Acceptance Criteria:
- ai-compose.yml.j2 Ansible template with 7 services
- triage-service: port 8090, volumes for drain3_state and rca_history.db
- ollama: port 11434, runtime:nvidia for GPU passthrough, model storage volume
- prometheus-mcp: port 8091, PROMETHEUS_URL from group_vars
- loki-mcp: port 8092, LOKI_URL from group_vars
- jaeger-mcp: port 8093, JAEGER_URL from group_vars
- drain3-mcp: port 8094, connects to triage Drain3 state
- rca-history-mcp: port 8095, reads triage SQLite volume
- nvidia-container-toolkit installed on AI VM
- Health checks for all 7 services
- Ansible host group ai added; playbooks/ai.yml deploys full stack

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-10: Decision history — /decisions endpoint + RCA History MCP (:8095) + dashboard
column30:
As a developer, I can view all past RCA decisions so that the demo shows decision history and audit trail.

Acceptance Criteria:
- SQLite at /data/rca_history.db (volume-mounted) with full schema
- GET /decisions returns last N (default 50), sorted by timestamp desc
- GET /decisions?alert_name=X filters by alert name
- RCA History MCP on port 8095: get_recent_rcas(hours), search_rcas(alert_name, days), get_rca_detail(rca_id), get_alert_frequency(alert_name, days)
- Optional: /dashboard HTML page showing decision log as styled table

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-09: 5-minute timeout fallback — raw alert passthrough (safety gate)
column30:
As an on-call engineer, if the LLM takes longer than 5 minutes I still receive the raw alert so that the AI layer never silently swallows an alert.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Entire pipeline in asyncio.wait_for(timeout=300)
- On TimeoutError: raw alert email with subject [ALERT] TIMEOUT: alert_name
- Timeout logged with alertname, elapsed time, pipeline stage at timeout
- Stored in RCA History as triage_decision: timeout_passthrough
- Implemented and tested BEFORE LLM integration
- Test: mock Ollama with 6-min sleep then verify raw alert email at exactly 5 min

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-08: ESCALATE action — send RCA email via SMTP
column30:
As an on-call engineer, when the LLM decides ESCALATE I receive an email with the full RCA so that I have actionable context without opening any dashboard.

Acceptance Criteria:
- On ESCALATE: email via smtplib with Gmail SMTP (smtp.gmail.com:587, TLS)
- Subject: [ALERT] severity: alert_name — affected_service
- Body: alert name, firing time, severity, LLM RCA report, suggested actions, confidence, evidence, dashboard links, RCA history context
- On DISMISS: no email, decision logged with reasoning
- Every decision stored in SQLite RCA History with full schema
- SMTP credentials via env vars
- SMTP failure does not crash pipeline — logged, decision still stored
- Test: trigger ESCALATE then verify email received with all fields

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-07: LLM prompt template + ESCALATE/DISMISS decision parser
column30:
As an on-call engineer, I receive ESCALATE or DISMISS with a written RCA so that I know exactly what action to take.

Acceptance Criteria:
- System prompt defines SRE assistant role with clear investigation instructions
- Prompt includes: alert metadata, pre-gathered context (metrics + Drain3-annotated logs + traces), MCP tool definitions for all 5 bridges
- LLM responds with JSON: decision, severity, reason, rca, anomaly_summary, suggested_actions, evidence
- Pydantic LLMDecision validates output — decision enum: ESCALATE or DISMISS
- On parse failure: retry once with stricter instruction, then default ESCALATE (safe fallback)
- Raw LLM response always logged regardless of parse success
- Queries Ollama at :11434/api/chat with MCP tool definitions — LLM autonomously calls tools
- Cross-pillar instruction in prompt
- Investigation duration logged

Story Points: 8
Estimate: 1.5 days
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-06: Parallel context fan-out — Prometheus + Loki + Jaeger
column30:
As the triage service, I can concurrently query all three pillars so that context gathering stays under 10 seconds.

Acceptance Criteria:
- asyncio.gather queries Prometheus (query_range, T-15m), Loki (search_logs, 50 lines), Jaeger (find_traces, 20 limit) concurrently
- Per-source timeout: 8 seconds — on timeout, partial context with note
- Total gathering under 10 seconds
- Loki lines passed through Drain3 analyze() for annotation
- Jaeger data includes span names, durations, error flags, trace_ids
- Prometheus data includes metric values, timestamps, labels
- If all sources timeout: LLM still invoked with alert metadata + MCP tools (degraded mode)
- Logged with per-source latency: prometheus_ms, loki_ms, jaeger_ms, total_ms

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-05: Alert deduplication + 5-min grouping window
column30:
As an operator, repeated firings of the same alert within 5 minutes are deduplicated so that Ollama is not called 20 times for one flapping alert.

Acceptance Criteria:
- In-memory dict keyed by (alertname, instance) with TTL = 5 minutes
- Duplicate within window: LLM call skipped, logged as deduplicated with window_remaining_seconds
- New or expired: proceeds to full pipeline
- Thread-safe with asyncio.Lock
- Resolve + re-fire within window: TTL resets, alert re-processed
- All dedup events logged with alertname, instance, action, duplicate count
- Test: 10 identical alerts in 5 min results in exactly 1 LLM invocation, 9 deduplicated

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-04: FastAPI scaffold — webhook receivers, async processing, health endpoint
column30:
As a developer, I can POST an alert webhook to /webhook/grafana and receive 202 Accepted immediately so that Grafana never times out.

Acceptance Criteria:
- POST /webhook/grafana accepts Grafana webhook JSON, returns 202 within 100ms
- POST /webhook/drain3 accepts Drain3 notification JSON, returns 202 within 100ms
- Both process async via FastAPI BackgroundTasks
- Pydantic models: GrafanaWebhook and Drain3Webhook with validation
- Invalid payloads return 422 with validation errors
- GET /health returns status, uptime, version
- GET /decisions returns last N decisions (default 50)
- Structured JSON logging for all requests
- Dockerfile with env vars: PROMETHEUS_URL, LOKI_URL, JAEGER_URL, OLLAMA_URL, SMTP_HOST, SMTP_USER, SMTP_PASSWORD, NOTIFICATION_EMAIL

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)
Blocks: Track B3

column1: E2-15: Drain3 observability — /stats endpoint + Prometheus metrics + MCP (:8094)
column30:
As an operator, I can see Drain3 cluster statistics and anomaly rates so that I can validate the anomaly detection layer.

Acceptance Criteria:
- GET /drain3/stats returns: total_clusters, recent_anomaly_rate, top_new_patterns, last_snapshot_time, baseline_age_seconds
- Prometheus metrics: drain3_clusters_total, drain3_anomalies_total, drain3_log_lines_processed_total, drain3_baseline_age_seconds
- Triage service added as Prometheus scrape target
- Grafana alert for drift detection (50+ new clusters/hour or anomaly rate <0.1% for 30min)
- Drain3 MCP on port 8094: get_clusters(), get_anomaly_rate(), match_log(line), get_baseline_info()

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-14: Drain3 + Loki integration — annotate log lines in LLM context
column30:
As the triage service, I can stream recent logs through Drain3 and get anomaly scores so that the LLM context includes which log lines are novel vs routine.

Acceptance Criteria:
- During context gathering, every Loki log line passed through Drain3 analyze()
- Each line prefixed: [ANOMALY] (new/rare, <5 matches) or [KNOWN] (established, >=5 matches)
- Anomaly summary block in LLM context: X of Y lines anomalous, N new patterns, top templates
- Annotated logs appear in prompt before MCP tool definitions
- LLM can also query Drain3 MCP (:8094) for deeper analysis
- Test: inject novel log pattern then verify it appears as [ANOMALY] in assembled prompt

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-13: Drain3 setup — log template mining + persistence
column30:
As the triage service, I have an unsupervised anomaly detection layer so that novel or rare log patterns are flagged before the LLM sees them.

Acceptance Criteria:
- drain3 pip package initialized within triage service process
- drain3.ini: similarity threshold ~0.4, max clusters, depth tuned for Spring Boot and Kong logs
- FileStatePersistence with volume mount — state survives restarts
- On startup: fetch last 1h of logs from Loki to seed baseline (first run only)
- analyze(log_line) returns: cluster_id, template, is_new_pattern, change_type
- S3 periodic snapshots of Drain3 state (configurable, default: daily)
- Known-good baseline tagging after confirmed healthy period
- Automated weekly reset from latest known-good snapshot
- Background log ingestion loop polling Loki every 30s

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-17: Cross-pillar correlation — metrics, logs, traces
column30:
As the LLM, I can correlate data across metrics, logs, and traces within the same time window so that my root cause analysis connects quantitative signals, qualitative evidence, and request-level context.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Time-based correlation: context gathering queries all three pillars using same window (T-15m to T)
- Trace-log correlation: LLM extracts trace_id from Loki log then calls get_trace via Jaeger MCP
- Trace-metric correlation: LLM identifies failing service from error spans then queries Prometheus
- Log-metric correlation: Drain3 anomalous logs trigger Prometheus check for coinciding anomalies
- LLM prompt includes instruction to always check all three pillars before verdict
- RCA output includes evidence section listing metrics, log lines, trace spans used
- Demo: trigger latency issue then show LLM correlating Prometheus spike + Loki errors + Jaeger slow spans

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-16: Jaeger MCP server (:8093)
column30:
As the LLM, I can query Jaeger through an MCP bridge so that I can autonomously search distributed traces during investigation.

Acceptance Criteria:
- FastAPI server on port 8093 on AI/LLM VM
- find_traces(service, operation, start, end, limit, tags): search by service and time window
- get_trace(trace_id): full trace with all spans, durations, status codes, parent-child relationships
- get_services(): list all traced services
- get_operations(service): list operations for a service
- No write endpoints — read-only
- JAEGER_URL configurable via env var
- GET /health returns healthy status with jaeger_reachable check
- Trace data includes trace_id for cross-referencing with Loki logs

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-03: Loki MCP server (:8092)
column30:
As the LLM, I can query Loki through an MCP bridge so that I can autonomously search logs during investigation.

Acceptance Criteria:
- FastAPI server on port 8092 on AI/LLM VM
- query_logs(logql, start, end, limit): returns matching log lines, max 50 per call
- get_label_values(label): returns available values for a label
- get_log_volume(logql, start, end): returns log line count per time bucket
- No write endpoints — read-only
- LOKI_URL configurable via env var
- GET /health returns healthy status with loki_reachable check
- 50-line cap enforced regardless of limit parameter

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-02: Prometheus MCP server (:8091)
column30:
As the LLM, I can query Prometheus through an MCP bridge so that I can autonomously gather metric data during investigation.

Acceptance Criteria:
- FastAPI server on port 8091 on AI/LLM VM
- query_instant(promql): accepts PromQL, returns instant vector
- query_range(promql, start, end, step): returns time series matrix
- get_alerts(): returns currently firing alerts with labels, state, value
- get_targets(): returns scrape targets with health status
- No write endpoints — read-only
- Every query logged (timestamp, PromQL, response time) in structured JSON
- PROMETHEUS_URL configurable via env var
- GET /health returns healthy status with prometheus_reachable check

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)

column1: E2-01: Configure Grafana Alerting webhook — remove Alertmanager
column30:
As an on-call engineer, alerts from Grafana Alerting are routed exclusively through the AI triage pipeline so that no alert reaches me without AI evaluation.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Grafana Alerting enabled with single webhook contact point: http://<ai-vm>:8090/webhook/grafana
- Alert rules migrated: high latency (p95 > 500ms/5m), high error rate (5xx > 5%/3m), service down (up == 0/2m), OTel span drop rate (> 1%)
- No email, Slack, or PagerDuty contact points in Grafana
- Alertmanager container removed from monitoring-compose.yml.j2
- Alertmanager Ansible role references removed
- Prometheus config alerting section removed
- Manual alert trigger results in webhook POST to :8090 within 2 min
- Webhook payload matches Grafana Alerting JSON format

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI RCA System (MVP)
Blocks: E2-06

column1: E1-05: End-to-end cloud validation — all services healthy, dashboards live
column30:
As a developer, I can open Grafana on the AWS monitoring VM and see all dashboards with live data so that the demo environment is validated. This is the Epic 1 completion gate.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Prometheus targets: all scrape targets UP
- Grafana unified-overview dashboard: all panels populated
- Loki returning logs from both services
- Jaeger traces visible with spans from Kong to Spring Boot to MySQL
- Ollama responding to inference queries within 30s
- load-test.sh generates visible traffic in all three pillars
- React frontend loads via CloudFront
- API responds through Kong with 200
- Spring Boot actuator metrics scraped through Kong

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AWS Demo Environment
Milestone: Epic 1 complete
column54: Epic 1 -- AWS Infrastructure Provisioning and Configuration

column1: E1-04: Ollama + NVIDIA drivers on GPU instance
column30:
As a developer, I can query Ollama at http://<ai-vm>:11434 so that LLM inference is available for the triage service.

Acceptance Criteria:
- nvidia-smi shows Tesla T4 GPU with 16 GB VRAM
- CUDA toolkit installed (nvcc --version returns version)
- Ollama running as systemd service on port 11434
- ollama list shows llama3.1:8b (Q4 quantized, <=5 GB)
- curl localhost:11434/api/generate returns valid response within 30s
- Ollama accessible from other VMs in private subnet
- Ansible role ollama/ created for repeatable setup
- GPU memory usage visible during inference (model on GPU, not CPU fallback)

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AWS Demo Environment
Blocks: E2-07
column54: Epic 1 -- AWS Infrastructure Provisioning and Configuration

column1: E1-03: Ansible deploy to AWS — Spring Boot, React, Kong, observability stack
column30:
As an operator, I can run ansible-playbook playbooks/site.yml against the AWS instances so that the full observability stack deploys exactly as it does locally.

Acceptance Criteria:
- inventory/production.yml updated with Terraform output IPs and RDS endpoint
- ansible-playbook playbooks/site.yml completes successfully (exit code 0, no failed tasks)
- Spring Boot running on backend EC2 (port 80 to 8080), connected to RDS MySQL endpoint
- React production build on S3 accessible via CloudFront URL
- Kong routes: /api and /actuator to backend EC2, / to CloudFront
- group_vars/all.yml endpoints updated to monitoring EC2 private IP
- Prometheus targets page: all scrape targets UP
- Grafana dashboards populated with live data
- Loki receiving logs from Spring Boot and Kong
- Jaeger showing traces for both services
- curl http://<network-ec2>:8000/api returns 200

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: AWS Demo Environment
column54: Epic 1 -- AWS Infrastructure Provisioning and Configuration

column1: E1-02: Terraform — VPC, 3+1 EC2s, RDS, S3+CloudFront
column30:
As an operator, I can run terraform apply to provision the full AWS demo environment so that infrastructure is repeatable and version-controlled.

Acceptance Criteria:
- terraform apply completes without errors in us-east-1
- VPC created with public subnet (CloudFront, NAT Gateway, bastion SSH) and private subnet (all EC2s, RDS)
- Monitoring EC2: t3.large (2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM), 50 GB gp3 EBS, private subnet
- Backend EC2: t3.small (2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM), 20 GB gp3 EBS, private subnet
- Network EC2: t3.small (2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM), 20 GB gp3 EBS, private subnet
- AI/LLM EC2: g4dn.xlarge (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, T4 GPU), 50 GB gp3 EBS, private subnet
- RDS: db.t3.micro MySQL, single-AZ, 20 GB gp3, accessible only from sg-backend
- S3 bucket with CloudFront distribution created
- Security groups match spec: sg-monitoring, sg-backend, sg-network, sg-ai, sg-rds
- Elastic IPs assigned to all EC2 instances
- terraform output produces: all instance IPs, RDS endpoint, CloudFront domain, S3 bucket name
- All resources tagged project:observability-rca
- SSH access works via bastion using key pair from E1-01
- terraform destroy cleanly removes all resources

Story Points: 8
Estimate: 1.5 days
Epic: AWS Demo Environment
Blocks: E1-03
column54: Epic 1 -- AWS Infrastructure Provisioning and Configuration

column1: E1-01: AWS account, IAM, billing alerts, SSH keys
column30:
As a team member, I can authenticate to AWS with scoped IAM credentials so that I have least-privilege access.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AWS account created and accessible by all team members via IAM login URL
- IAM users created per team member with MFA enforced and permissions scoped to EC2, RDS, S3, CloudFront, VPC, EBS (no AdministratorAccess)
- CloudWatch billing alarms configured at $100, $200, $300 thresholds with SNS email notification to team lead
- Tagging policy established: all resources tagged project:observability-rca
- SSH key pairs generated (ed25519 or rsa-4096) and securely distributed — private keys never committed to git
- Account root user secured with MFA and not used for daily operations

Story Points: 2
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AWS Demo Environment
Blocks: all other E1 stories
column54: Epic 1 -- AWS Infrastructure Provisioning and Configuration
